summary:
Mediterranean Trade Networks (c. 500–100 BC) were the maritime and overland exchange systems connecting the civilizations of the Mediterranean Basin — Greek, Phoenician/Carthaginian, Roman, Etruscan, Egyptian, and Levantine — which moved agricultural products, metals, luxury goods, and slaves across one of the world's most productive commercial zones and provided the economic foundation for classical civilization.

The Mediterranean's enclosed geography, with reliable seasonal winds and numerous harbors, made it ideal for maritime commerce. The major trading hubs shifted over this period: Phoenician and Carthaginian merchants dominated western Mediterranean trade in the early period (controlling Spain, Sardinia, and North Africa), while Greek traders operated from colonies across the Mediterranean rim. By the 3rd–2nd centuries BC, Rhodes had become the premier commercial hub of the eastern Mediterranean, with sophisticated maritime law, financial instruments, and insurance practices. Roman conquest gradually subordinated these independent commercial networks to Roman administration, but did not disrupt them. Key commodities included: grain (Sicily and Egypt to Greece and Rome), olive oil and wine (Greece, Italy, and Spain across the whole basin), metals (Spain and Britain), timber (from Macedon and the Black Sea coast), and slaves (from all points). The Roman peace (Pax Romana) after 27 BC would accelerate this already vigorous commerce.
